Wellington, FL 33414

citations.narrative:
There is no documentation maintained to indicate that an annual review of the contingency plan has been completed. The
contingency plan must be reviewed by the facility on at least an annual basis to ensure that it adequately addresses all
required contingency-related criteria and that no updates or changes need to be made to the plan such as changes made
as a result of any recent unforeseen circumstance or the addition of new species. The facility must maintain
documentation of their contingency plan annual reviews including documenting any amendments or changes made since
the previous year's review. Correct by March 27, 2026

Sliced papaya and honeydew melon used for feeding enrichment were observed stored in a refrigerator without being
placed in a leakproof container with a tightly fitting lid. The refrigerator was also observed to be dirty, with dried brown
residue and crumbs present on the shelves. Improper storage of food items may affect the nutritive value and palatability
of the food. Open supplies of food must be kept in leakproof containers with tightly fitting lids to prevent spoilage and
contamination. Correct by: 3/12/2026

citations.desc: Cleaning, sanitization, housekeeping, and pest control.
citations.narrative:
The facility does not currently maintain an effective pest control program, as evidenced by the large number of rodent
droppings throughout the room housing a black and white ruffed lemur. Rodent droppings were present on the floor and
inside the enclosure. Additionally, large numbers of fruit flies were observed in and around the food receptacle. Pests
such as rodents and fruit flies may spread disease and contaminate food, surfaces, and animal housing areas.
An effective program for control of insects, external parasites affecting nonhuman primates, and birds and mammals that
are pests, must be established and maintained so as to promote the health and well-being of the animals and reduce
contamination by pests in animal areas. Correct by: 4/06/2026

The wooden shelter located within an outdoor enclosure housing 5 goats was observed to be in disrepair. The structure
was leaning to the right and contained large areas of deteriorated and damaged wood. Failure to maintain housing
facilities in good repair can result in injury or harm to the animals. Housing facilities shall be structurally sound and shall
be maintained in good repair to protect the animals from injury and to contain the animals within.

At the time of the inspection, only one employee was present to feed animals and clean enclosures for 45 regulated
animals. This same employee is also responsible for conducting guided tours on a daily basis. A facility representative
stated that there is one volunteer who works approximately two hours per week; however, the facility does not have plans
to hire additional staff.
The non-compliances identified during this inspection, as well as those identified on previous inspection reports, are
consistent with inadequate staffing levels. Without a sufficient number of adequately trained employees, the facility cannot
provide the husbandry practices necessary to maintain the animals in a manner that ensures their health and welfare.
A sufficient number of adequately trained employees shall be utilized to maintain the professionally acceptable level of
husbandry practices set forth in this subpart. Such practices shall be under a supervisor who has a background in animal
care. Correct by April 6, 2026.

Multiple ducks have been found deceased within the same outdoor primary enclosure. On March 27, 2025, a Pekin duck
was found deceased in the primary enclosure by a facility representative with wounds consistent with predation. On
December 15, 2025, an additional Pekin duck was found deceased in the same primary enclosure with wounds also
consistent with predation. Following the initial incident, no modifications were made to the enclosure to restrict other
animals from entering. Primary enclosures that do not contain the birds securely and/or restrict other animals from
entering can negatively affect the welfare of the birds contained within, such as animals escaping, being predated upon,
injured, etc. Primary enclosures must be constructed and maintained so that they restrict other animals from entering the
enclosure. Correct by March 20, 2026.
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with a facility representative.
Additional Inspectors:
KRISTIN NAPOLI, SUPERVISORY ANIMAL CARE SPECIALIST n

narrative:
2.134(b) 
   
Contingency planning. 
There is no documentation maintained to indicate that an annual review of the contingency plan has been completed. The 
contingency plan must be reviewed by the facility on at least an annual basis to ensure that it adequately addresses all 
required contingency-related criteria and that no updates or changes need to be made to the plan such as changes made 
as a result of any recent unforeseen circumstance or the addition of new species. The facility must maintain 
documentation of their contingency plan annual reviews including documenting any amendments or changes made since 
the previous year's review. Correct by March 27, 2026 
3.75(e) 
   
Housing facilities, general. 
Sliced papaya and honeydew melon used for feeding enrichment were observed stored in a refrigerator without being 
placed in a leakproof container with a tightly fitting lid. The refrigerator was also observed to be dirty, with dried brown 
residue and crumbs present on the shelves. Improper storage of food items may affect the nutritive value and palatability 
of the food. Open supplies of food must be kept in leakproof containers with tightly fitting lids to prevent spoilage and 
contamination. Correct by: 3/12/2026 
3.84(d) 
   
Cleaning, sanitization, housekeeping, and pest control. 
The facility does not currently maintain an effective pest control program, as evidenced by the large number of rodent 
droppings throughout the room housing a black and white ruffed lemur. Rodent droppings were present on the floor and 
inside the enclosure. Additionally, large numbers of fruit flies were observed in and around the food receptacle. Pests 
such as rodents and fruit flies may spread disease and contaminate food, surfaces, and animal housing areas. 
 
An effective program for control of insects, external parasites affecting nonhuman primates, and birds and mammals that 
are pests, must be established and maintained so as to promote the health and well-being of the animals and reduce 
contamination by pests in animal areas. Correct by: 4/06/2026 
3.125(a)  Repeat

Facilities, general. 
The wooden shelter located within an outdoor enclosure housing 5 goats was observed to be in disrepair. The structure 
was leaning to the right and contained large areas of deteriorated and damaged wood. Failure to maintain housing 
facilities in good repair can result in injury or harm to the animals. Housing facilities shall be structurally sound and shall 
be maintained in good repair to protect the animals from injury and to contain the animals within. 
3.132 
   
Employees. 
At the time of the inspection, only one employee was present to feed animals and clean enclosures for 45 regulated 
animals. This same employee is also responsible for conducting guided tours on a daily basis. A facility representative 
stated that there is one volunteer who works approximately two hours per week; however, the facility does not have plans 
to hire additional staff.  
 
The non-compliances identified during this inspection, as well as those identified on previous inspection reports, are 
consistent with inadequate staffing levels. Without a sufficient number of adequately trained employees, the facility cannot 
provide the husbandry practices necessary to maintain the animals in a manner that ensures their health and welfare.  
 
A sufficient number of adequately trained employees shall be utilized to maintain the professionally acceptable level of 
husbandry practices set forth in this subpart. Such practices shall be under a supervisor who has a background in animal 
care. Correct by April 6, 2026. 
3.153(a)(1)(iv)  Critical
   
Primary enclosures. 
Multiple ducks have been found deceased within the same outdoor primary enclosure. On March 27, 2025, a Pekin duck 
was found deceased in the primary enclosure by a facility representative with wounds consistent with predation. On 
December 15, 2025, an additional Pekin duck was found deceased in the same primary enclosure with wounds also 
consistent with predation. Following the initial incident, no modifications were made to the enclosure to restrict other 
animals from entering. Primary enclosures that do not contain the birds securely and/or restrict other animals from 
entering can negatively affect the welfare of the birds contained within, such as animals escaping, being predated upon, 
injured, etc. Primary enclosures must be constructed and maintained so that they restrict other animals from entering the 
enclosure. Correct by March 20, 2026. 
 
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with a facility representative. 
 
Additional Inspectors: 
KRISTIN NAPOLI, SUPERVISORY ANIMAL CARE SPECIALIST n
